title: Using podman rootful, but mapping the container to a user and keeping groups
body:
**Description**
I am trying to understand starting podman as a rootful user (to get overlay2 access) but still trying to start containers as a specific unprivileged host user, which inside the container is root. 

Additionally, this `user` belongs to a common group that I also want in the container so if the common group has read/write permissions on a host file, it can read/write to it from the container. 

How can this be done? 

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

First My user1 setup:  

```
user1 uid = 1002
user1 gid = 1004
userCommon gid = 1002
```

1. 
```
sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:1001 --gidmap 1002:1002:1 --gidmap 1003:101003:65536  -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>
```

This should be mapping user1:user1 as `root` into the container. 
User namespace filling for 1:100000:65536 for UID. 
User namespace filling for 1:100000:1001 for GID. 
Map the common group `userCommon` directly into the container at 1002. 
Then user namespace filling for 1003:101003:65536. 

2. 
```
# cat /tmp/groupCommonRead.txt
cat: /tmp/groupCommonRead.txt: Permission denied
```

**Describe the results you received:**
Permission denied when attempting to read a file that is control by a common group permission. 

**Describe the results you expected:**
I should be able to read from the file as my user that is running container belongs in the common group, and I have mapped the common group directly into the container. 


Now I imagine this is related because the group inside the container has no information about the users in the commonGroup? Is that right? 
Is there any way I can properly map this group into the container such that the common group can be read from inside the container, and remain read by other users on the host? 


**A version I did get to work**, was using: `--group-add keep-groups` 

```
sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --group-add keep-groups  -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>
```

But this required me to add my host `root` to the `userCommon ` group because that is the user that is running podman. 

Is this the only approach that would work?

comments.body:
since you run as root, my suggestion is to add the `userCommon` group to the user namespace:

`sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:$userCommonGID:1  -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it` and if you run with `CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE` as your command does, you won't even need to set the group or additional groups

@giuseppe Unfortunately your suggestion seems not to have worked either: 

```
newuser@tegra-ubuntu:~$sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>
root@f3ea5d275add:/# cat /tmp/group.txt
cat: /tmp/group.txt: Permission denied
root@f3ea5d275add:/# ls -la /tmp/group.txt
-rw-r----- 1 nobody  100000 5 May 11 22:27 /tmp/group.txt
root@f3ea5d275add:/# exit
```

```
newuser@tegra-ubuntu:~$ id
uid=1002(newuser) gid=1004(newuser) groups=1004(newuser),27(sudo),44(video),1002(userCommon)
newuser@tegra-ubuntu:~$ cat /tmp/group.txt
test
```

```
# ls -la /tmp/group.txt
-rw-r----- 1 nobody  100000 5 May 11 22:27 /tmp/group.txt
```

this should not happen, it should be owned by a group that is mapped into the user namespace.

Can you show me the output for this command on the host (not in a container):

```
# ls -ln /tmp/group.txt
```

and make sure that group is mapped inside the user namespace

```
newuser@tegra-ubuntu:~$ ls -ln /tmp/group.txt
-rw-r----- 1 1001 1002 5 May 11 22:27 /tmp/group.txt
```

ok so `DAC_OVERRIDE` still fails when the uid is not mapped in the user namespace.

In this case I think you can fix it by adding `--group-add 1002` to the `podman run` options

Could you provide more details? @giuseppe 

If I keep your suggestion the same and do this:

```
newuser@tegra-ubuntu:~$ sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 --group-add 1002 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>
groups: cannot find name for group ID 1002
root@4f71e387835f:/# cat /tmp/group.txt
cat: /tmp/group.txt: Permission denied
```
I get the warning: `groups: cannot find name for group ID 1002`
And Permission denied.


If I drop the --gidmap and do this:

```
sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --group-add 1002 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>
groups: cannot find name for group ID 1002
root@ed2458c54850:/# cat /tmp/group.txt
cat: /tmp/group.txt: Permission denied
```
I get the warning: `groups: cannot find name for group ID 1002`
And Permission denied.


And lastly, if I try what I did in my ticket opening:

```
sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:1001 --group-add 1002 --gidmap 1003:101003:65536  -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it  <image>
Error: OCI runtime error: setgroups: Invalid argument
```
`
Error: OCI runtime error: setgroups: Invalid argument
`

I was able to get it to work if I also mapped in the `user` like you suggested. 

```
newuser@tegra-ubuntu:~$ sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --uidmap 100000:1001:1 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>
root@5768bc089b0d:/# cat /tmp/group.txt
test
```

However, I was hoping not to have to do this.

sorry, try:

`sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 --group-add 100000 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>`

Thanks @giuseppe. 

Looks like it worked!  But I am confused! 

```
sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 --group-add 100000 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>
groups: cannot find name for group ID 100000
root@7983c0f2103f:/# cat /tmp/group.txt
test
```

I still get what seems to be a warning of: `groups: cannot find name for group ID 100000` 

But If I remove the ` --group-add 100000`

```
sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>
root@ce6be019a8a0:/# cat /tmp/group.txt
cat: /tmp/group.txt: Permission denied
```
But no warning, haha.  

Why does this fix it? Is it because group 100000 exists in the namespace? 
But the warning is from checking the host namespace?

Also @giuseppe 

Just for my understanding, this is essentially equivalent to the docker re map user mode, right? https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/userns-remap/ 

Do you know of any other way to do this in podman then what I am doing? 
I just want to verify my approach is correct, since there are many uid/gid flags in podman.

> I still get what seems to be a warning of: `groups: cannot find name for group ID 100000`

If you run groups, then it cannot find the ID 100000 in `/etc/group`.  This is an expected error.



> Just for my understanding, this is essentially equivalent to the docker re map user mode, right? https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/userns-remap/

right.

> Do you know of any other way to do this in podman then what I am doing?
> I just want to verify my approach is correct, since there are many uid/gid flags in podman.

I think the way you are doing it in the last command is the correct one.  With root you can map any ID so it makes sense to map exactly the group you need into the container
since it seems to be solved, I am closing the issue.  Please feel free to keep commenting

@giuseppe I have noticed that starting containers like this, seems to take the container extra long to start for an image of size ~600mb. 

**With** the `uid/gid mappings` it takes about: **2 minutes and 44 seconds**


**Without** the `uid/gid mappings` it takes about: **Less than 5 seconds.** 

Could you elaborate why this might be happening? 

I see that with it **with** the `uid/gid mappings` I see 2 new Filesystems created in my output of 


```
df -h
/dev/mapper/enc_mmcblk0p1   7.9G  6.8G  635M  92% /
/dev/mapper/enc_mmcblk0p21  7.6G  6.1G  1.1G  86% /data
none                         16G     0   16G   0% /dev
tmpfs                        16G  312K   16G   1% /dev/shm
shm                          63M     0   63M   0% /data/podman/users/newuser/podman-root/overlay-containers/bd83b4183449ef29f1902865e327521df7dae35d9189f90a526823fd72ba7b03/userdata/shm
overlay                     7.6G  6.1G  1.1G  86% /data/podman/users/newuser/podman-root/overlay/31b7e78da8ca10c2c274386913bb99321cf619a7b7e314ee681e0919f31f89aa/merged
````

The `shm` and `overlay` are new and go away when I close the container. 


Those filesystem do not appear when I start the container without the mappings.

> @giuseppe I have noticed that starting containers like this, seems to take the container extra long to start for an image of size ~600mb.
> 
> **With** the `uid/gid mappings` it takes about: **2 minutes and 44 seconds**
> 
> **Without** the `uid/gid mappings` it takes about: **Less than 5 seconds.**
> 
> Could you elaborate why this might be happening?

when a mapping is specified, containers/storage needs to create a clone of the image with the IDs shifted to what must be used in the user namespace.  Once idmapped mounts will support overlay we won't need this anymore

Using the image again with the same mapping, should be instantaneous.
Did you use metacopy?
```
grep metacopy /etc/containers/storage.conf
mountopt = "nodev,metacopy=on"
```

Hey @gabibeyer, @rhatdan Thanks for the continued help, I really appreciate it! 

Lets see a few questions. 

I added the CLI: 
```
--storage-opt=mountopt=nodev,metacopy=on
```

But when I do a podman info, I still see the status showing false:


```
  graphOptions:
    overlay.imagestore: /data/cloud/assets/yguysdfiudyf8yww3ebjhwei93hlowie
    overlay.mountopt: nodev,metacopy=on
  graphRoot: /data/podman/users/newuser/podman-root
  graphStatus:
    Backing Filesystem: extfs
    Native Overlay Diff: "false"
    Supports d_type: "true"
    Using metacopy: "false"
```

1. Not sure if I am missing a step? 
Realistically my kernel could be too old for that feature (maybe I am wrong).
I am on **4.9** and that won't be changing, that is why I am trying this `sudo` with `uid/gid mapping approach` 


I do have another follow up question @giuseppe and @rhatdan.

2. If my plan is to only run the image one time, as a single user.

Can I pull the image down as the user that I am planning to run so that no "clone of the image with the IDs shifted to what must be used in the user namespace" happens? 
Maybe if I pulled the image without root, could that speed this up? 
The challenge is I need to still the pull the image with `root` because I need to use `overlay2`. 
I would be fine to manually change the permission of the files if that would work. 

3. Is there anywhere I can read more about: mountopt = "nodev,metacopy=on" ?

> ```
> 
> ```

yes that could be the reason, that kernel is quite old.



>     1. If my plan is to only run the image one time, as a single user.
> 
> 
> Can I pull the image down as the user that I am planning to run so that no "clone of the image with the IDs shifted to what must be used in the user namespace" happens?
> Maybe if I pulled the image without root, could that speed this up?
> The challenge is I need to still the pull the image with `root` because I need to use `overlay2`.
> I would be fine to manually change the permission of the files if that would work.

does it work if you let the run command pull the image instead of pulling it beforehand?  Please make sure to pass the mapping you want to use.

>     1. Is there anywhere I can read more about: mountopt = "nodev,metacopy=on" ?

you can find more info here: https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/filesystems/overlayfs.html?highlight=overlayfs

>does it work if you let the run command pull the image instead of pulling it beforehand? Please make sure to pass the mapping you want to use.

It seems to be a quicker, yes. But it still takes about: 40 seconds after `Storing signatures` log line:


```
Trying to pull image.....
Copying blob c4f4e0f882d3 done
Copying blob a199cb2dd71e done
Copying blob 3e956de9ea4b done
Copying blob e26b78d1aaed done
Copying blob ac42496d0bc2 done
Copying blob 7db2983f5802 done
Copying config 10faffedd5 done
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
root@73a9a799a038:/#
```


However, I would be looking for a solution that allows me to pull the image and run separately for my use case. 
Do you think if I change the permissions manually of the images/layers there is a path forward?

I just tested it! 

If I manually change all the permissions of the image files/directories to my user, it works quite quickly! This is great! :D 

```
sudo chown -R 1002:1004 <path-to-image>
```

It still makes the filesystems:

```
df -h
Filesystem                  Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/mapper/enc_mmcblk0p1   7.9G  6.8G  639M  92% /
/dev/mapper/enc_mmcblk0p21  7.6G  5.0G  2.2G  70% /data
shm                          63M     0   63M   0% /data/podman/users/newuser/podman-root/overlay-containers/1b96af876c72e5efbad09e62930de42bfdf95b9d95a8ffd4c60cac8ea751ec84/userdata/shm
overlay                     7.6G  5.0G  2.2G  70% /data/podman/users/newuser/podman-root/overlay/e821272ef3fd490da907f3813978213bd82352b0ade2f105e69e350800969287/merged
```

But only takes ~5 seconds!

@giuseppe does it make sense that if I chown the image files permissions to the user it happens instantaneous? 
I am surprised it still doesn't need to do the cloning? 

Also,

> Once idmapped mounts will support overlay we won't need this anymore

Is there a tracking issue for this?

> > Once idmapped mounts will support overlay we won't need this anymore
> 
> Is there a tracking issue for this?

no that must happen in the kernel first

> If I manually change all the permissions of the image files/directories to my user, it works quite quickly! This is great! :D
> 
> ```
> sudo chown -R 1002:1004 <path-to-image>
> ```

what is `/path/to/image`?

`<path-to-image>` is my `--root` option on the cli. @giuseppe 
 
 
 So if I pull an `ubuntu` image with `--root` in location: `/data/podman-read-only` 

`sudo podman --root /data/podman-read-only pull ubuntu`
 
 Then I do: 
 
 `sudo chown -R 1002:1004 /data/podman-read-only`
 
 I can then do: 
 
 `sudo podman --root /data/podman/some-path --storage-opt=additionalimagestore=/data/podman-read-only run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it ubuntu`
 
 And it will start immediately because the mapping is already correct. 
 
 But if I leave the  `/data/podman-read-only` as `root` it takes 2+ mins to start.

`/data/podman-read-only`

where is this path stored?  Network file system?





> Is there a way I can view the cloned image files that exist in the username space?

Unfortunately I think we don't expose this information, but it is available only in the local storage DB

> /data/podman-read-only
where is this path stored? Network file system?

It is saved on my host, just a different disk, and it is using overlay with podman

>but it is available only in the local storage DB

This is the bolt_state.db ?

@giuseppe @rhatdan 
If I start a 2nd container with the same mappings, is that safe to do so? 

Container 1:
```
sudo podman --root /data/podman/some-path --storage-opt=additionalimagestore=/data/podman-read-only run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it ubuntu
```

Container 2:
```
sudo podman --root /data/podman/some-path --storage-opt=additionalimagestore=/data/podman-read-only run --uidmap 0:2003:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:2004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it ubuntu
```

(All that changed between 1 and 2 is the "root" user in the container). 

Or do I need to make sure container 2 changes the mapping for me as well? 

```
sudo podman --root /data/podman/some-path --storage-opt=additionalimagestore=/data/podman-read-only run --uidmap 0:2003:1 --uidmap 1:165536:65536 --gidmap 0:2004:1 --gidmap 1:165536:65536 --gidmap 165536:1002:1 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it ubuntu
```

Like this where I use: `--uidmap 1:165536:65536 ` and ` --gidmap 1:165536:65536 --gidmap 165536:1002:1 `

If the answer is "yes, you need to update the mappings for each container otherwise you will have user conflicts", 
I just want to know to verify if my approach of starting containers as non-root users with running podman as root seems correct. 

Does `--userns=auto` make more sense for what I am trying? 
If I do use `--userns=auto` with gidmapping/uidmapping, how do you reccomend I do the above? 

Because I want to map a specific user and group to container root, which is simple enough because container ID should be 0. But what about for additional groups I may want mapped in that exist on the host? How do I know which ContainerID to set? Or does it not matter?

Ir works but you have less security because if a malicious process manages to escape a container then it has control on the files owned by the other container.  It is safer if the mappings are distinct and that is what the --userns=auto functionality is for

Thanks @giuseppe 

But how would I map additional groups if I was user --userns=auto?

Above you had me map group 1002 as: 

```
--gidmap 100000:1002:1
```
Because host is 100000 would be unused. 

What about for userns=auto? 
How do I pick an unused host is when I don't know which ID's auto is setting?
please look at the documentation for `userns=auto`.  There is a way to force mappings to be present with "gidmapping": `auto:gidmapping=...`

@giuseppe  do you mind providing me with more context on what this is doing? 

> sorry, try:
> 
> `sudo podman --runtime /usr/local/bin/crun run --uidmap 0:1002:1 --uidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 0:1004:1 --gidmap 1:100000:65536 --gidmap 100000:1002:1 --group-add 100000 -v /tmp:/tmp --rm -it <image>`

Specifically: 
>--gidmap 100000:1002:1 --group-add 100000

1. This maps group 1002 into the container at ID 100000, then `--group-add 100000` attaches this group to the user running the main container process. Is this accurate?  

2. How does this work with `read-only`? 
I have tested it, and tt works, but I don't understand where this `--group-add 100000` is stored? 
It does not appear under: `/etc/groups` in the container. 
I guess that is what this complaint is: `groups: cannot find name for group ID 100000`
So is this stored in memory? 

This is more for my understanding. 

Thanks!
